learningObjective: Understand that a tesseract is created by dragging a cube into the fourth dimension, and we can only see its 3D shadow/projection. The aha moment: the 4D construction process follows the exact same geometric logic as building a 3D cube from a 2D square — the pattern is identical, making 4D feel mechanically comprehensible.
ahaMoment: The student realizes that the 4D construction process is not mysterious—it follows the exact same geometric logic as building a 3D cube from a 2D square, just applied one dimension higher; the pattern is identical, making 4D feel mechanically comprehensible

miniSpec.title: Construction Recipe Comparison: 2D→3D vs 3D→4D
miniSpec.narrative_wrapper: On the left, you watch a square being dragged perpendicular to itself to sweep out a cube, with a progress slider showing each intermediate stage. On the right, the exact same slider simultaneously shows a cube being dragged through the fourth dimension to sweep out a tesseract. Every construction step happens in parallel, so you see the pattern: each dimension adds the same way.
miniSpec.manipulated_variable: Sweep distance controls how far the lower-dimensional shape has traveled perpendicular to itself, changing the completeness of the higher-dimensional object being constructed
miniSpec.primary_visual_feedback: Both panels animate synchronously: the left shows the square creating stacked copies connected by edges to form the cube; the right shows the cube creating stacked copies connected by faces to form the tesseract's structure
miniSpec.causal_chain_exposed: Numeric readouts show identical sweep distances; intermediate copies appear as ghosted layers; connecting elements (edges on left, faces on right) materialize progressively; labels count vertices, edges, faces, and cells in parallel
miniSpec.trace_strategy: The previous position of the swept shape remains visible as a faint ghost at the starting location, with all connecting elements persisting, creating a visual history of the sweeping motion and showing the complete path traveled
miniSpec.aha_moment: The student realizes that the 4D construction process is not mysterious—it follows the exact same geometric logic as building a 3D cube from a 2D square, just applied one dimension higher; the pattern is identical, making 4D feel mechanically comprehensible

modelVariables.tesseract4d_sweepProgress: Current sweep progress from 0 (initial shape) to 1 (fully extruded shape), controls BOTH visualizations simultaneously
modelVariables.tesseract4d_leftVertices: Derived: vertex count for left panel (square→cube): 4 at start, 8 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_leftEdges: Derived: edge count for left panel (square→cube): 4 at start, 12 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_leftFaces: Derived: face count for left panel (square→cube): 1 at start, 6 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_rightVertices: Derived: vertex count for right panel (cube→tesseract): 8 at start, 16 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_rightEdges: Derived: edge count for right panel (cube→tesseract): 12 at start, 32 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_rightFaces: Derived: face count for right panel (cube→tesseract): 6 at start, 24 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_rightCells: Derived: cell count for right panel (cube→tesseract): 0 at start, 8 at end
modelVariables.tesseract4d_hasInteracted: Boolean: whether the student has started dragging the sweep slider

assertions.description: On load, both shapes are at sweep=0, showing the starting shape with no extrusion, inviting the student to drag
assertions.visualCheck: Left panel shows flat square with 4 vertices; right panel shows cube with 8 vertices; sweep slider at 0; both labeled with starting counts

assertions.description: Dragging the sweep slider updates BOTH panels simultaneously with the same progress value
assertions.visualCheck: Both panels show halfway-extruded shapes; left cube forming with connecting edges; right tesseract forming with connecting faces; vertex counts doubled in both

assertions.description: At sweep=1, left shows complete cube (8 vertices, 12 edges, 6 faces) and right shows complete tesseract (16 vertices, 32 edges, 24 faces, 8 cells)
assertions.visualCheck: Left panel shows complete 3D cube wireframe; right panel shows complete tesseract projection; all counts match expected values

assertions.description: The pattern is clear: vertices double at each dimension (4→8 on left, 8→16 on right)
assertions.visualCheck: Both panels show vertex labels updating from 4→8 (left) and 8→16 (right), demonstrating the doubling pattern

assertions.description: Ghost traces of the swept shape remain visible, showing the construction path
assertions.visualCheck: Both panels show faint ghost copies of the original shape at starting position, with connecting elements tracing the sweep path

invariants.description: Left panel vertices match cube formula: 4 when sweep=0, 8 when sweep>0
Right panel vertices match tesseract formula: 8 when sweep=0, 16 when sweep>0
Both panels always share the same sweep progress value
